prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. This is from my colleague who teaches writing at the local university. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Strong narrative structure** - The essay has a clear beginning, middle, and end with good chronological flow. The story is easy to follow.

**Authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine and relatable. Lines like "Obviously" and the self-deprecating humor make the narrator likeable.

**Solid reflection** - The writer goes beyond just telling the story and actually extracts meaningful lessons, particularly the insight about "you don't know what you don't know."

**Specific details** - Marcus, the "stay out of the way" comment, and the transition to debate team give the essay concrete anchors rather than vague generalizations.

## What Needs Work:

**Show, don't tell** - The tryout scenes could be much more vivid. What did the gym smell like? What did the writer's heart feel like during the scrimmage? More sensory details would make this come alive.

**Tone consistency** - The conversational style works, but "So yeah" to start the final paragraph is too informal for academic writing. The tone could be relaxed without being quite so casual.

**Underdeveloped sections** - The pivot to debate feels rushed. This is actually an interesting part of the story that deserves more space—how did that decision happen? What was the first debate like?

**Sentence variety** - Many sentences follow simple subject-verb patterns. More complex sentence structures would elevate the prose.

**Depth of reflection** - The lessons are good but somewhat predictable. Push further into the emotional complexity or unexpected insights.

**Overall**: This is solid, competent work that fulfills the assignment but doesn't exceed expectations. With revision focusing on vivid detail and deeper exploration, it could easily move to an 8 or 9.

# Grade: 7/10

## What Works:

**Strong narrative structure** - The essay has a clear arc from confidence → humiliation → growth, and it flows logically from beginning to end.

**Authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine and makes the writer relatable. The honesty about being "in over my head" is refreshing.

**The debate team transition feels rushed** - This is an interesting turn that deserves more development. What specifically about that first debate meeting? This could be another vivid scene.

**Could go deeper emotionally** - The reflection is solid but somewhat surface-level. The ending insight about failure being "just information" is good but feels a bit neat/pat for the messiness described earlier.

**Overall:** This is a competent, honest personal narrative that fulfills the assignment. With more descriptive richness and emotional depth, it could move from good to excellent.

**Strong authentic voice** - The conversational tone feels genuine and makes the essay engaging. The writer sounds like a real person reflecting honestly on a painful memory.

**Recommendation:** This is a solid draft with good bones. Revision should focus on adding sensory details, varying sentence structure, and deepening the emotional landscape while maintaining that authentic voice.

The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

This is a solid, competent personal narrative that fulfills the assignment with genuine reflection. To move from good to excellent, it needs richer sensory detail and deeper emotional excavation of key moments.
